title: 78,557 Tech Jobs Gone to AI in Q1: What's Actually Happening
metaDescription: Nearly 40k tech jobs lost to AI in Q1 2026. The numbers are real. Here's what it means and why some people are still winning.
author: Marty @ CertFlo

sections.heading: The Numbers Are Real: AI Is Taking Jobs Right Now
sections.body: So there's this report from Nikkei Asia. 78,557 tech jobs gone in Q1 2026. Most of them in the US. But here's the thing that got my attention: almost 38,000 of those cuts were straight up AI replacements. Not budget cuts or market stuff. AI. This isn't some prediction anymore. It's happening. The data's right there at Techmeme.com if you wanna look. idk man, I've been watching this stuff for a while and this feels different. Faster. Like companies figured out they can just replace entire workflows instead of people.

sections.heading: Your Job Probably Isn't Safe Either
sections.body:
Think cause you're not in tech you're good? Nah. These tech companies are basically the testing ground. When they start cutting humans for AI, every other company is watching and taking notes.

Data entry people. Customer service. Junior analysts. Content moderators. Basic coding. Admin assistants. That's not just tech jobs dude. That's office jobs everywhere.

But here's what nobody's talking about: for every job AI kills, it's creating space for people who know how to use it. It's not humans vs AI. It's people who figured out AI vs people who didn't.

sections.heading: Some People Are Actually Winning
sections.body:
While 78,557 people lost their jobs, there's millions of others using AI to become impossible to replace. Managers cutting reporting from days to hours. Sales reps doubling their pipeline. Marketing teams doing 10x the content.

These aren't tech geniuses. Just regular office people who made one smart move: they learned to work with AI instead of pretending it doesn't exist.

The companies doing layoffs aren't eliminating all humans. They're eliminating humans who can't do more than what AI can do. The difference? AI skills.

sections.heading: The Window Is Closing
sections.body: Every month you wait is a month someone else gets ahead. The people surviving these layoffs and getting promoted? They started building their AI toolkit months ago. I've seen this before. When computers showed up, the typists who learned spreadsheets became analysts. When the internet hit, librarians who learned search became researchers. Now it's AI's turn. By 2027, every job's gonna assume you know AI. Question isn't whether you'll need these skills. Question is whether you'll have them before your next review. This isn't trying to scare you. It's just pattern recognition. The people who adapt early don't just survive. They end up running things. If you're ready to actually do something about it, there's a resource that gives you the practical stuff instead of just talking about it. Get the free FlowState guide at getfluxe.gumroad.com/l/FlowState and start building your AI advantage today. Have a beautiful day ! :)
